facts.size: About 25 cm (10 in) — similar in length to a starling
facts.habitat: Lawns, parks, open woodlands, and gardens
facts.migration: Short-distance migrant; some populations stay year-round in warmer areas

hints.region: Found year-round across North America, from Canada south to central Mexico
hints.size: About the size of a large starling — roughly 25 cm from bill to tail tip
hints.behavior: Famously runs across lawns in quick bursts, then stops and tilts its head to listen for earthworms underground

funFact: American Robins can produce up to three clutches of chicks in a single breeding season.

facts.size: 71–96 cm body length; wingspan up to 2.4 m
facts.habitat: Coasts, large lakes, and rivers with tall trees for nesting
facts.diet: Primarily fish, supplemented by waterfowl and carrion
facts.migration: Mostly resident; northern populations migrate south in winter

hints.region: Found near large bodies of water across North America, from Alaska to Florida
hints.size: One of North America's largest raptors — wingspan can reach over 2 metres
hints.behavior: Swoops low to snatch live fish from just below the water's surface with its powerful talons

funFact: Bald Eagles mate for life and return to the same nest each year, adding sticks until the nest can weigh over a tonne.

facts.size: About 25–30 cm, roughly the size of a pigeon
facts.habitat: Open ocean in winter; returns to grassy sea cliffs to breed in summer
facts.diet: Small fish, especially sand eels and herring
facts.migration: Spends most of the year far out at sea; comes ashore only to breed

hints.region: Nests on rocky sea cliffs of the North Atlantic — Iceland, Norway, and the British Isles hold huge colonies
hints.size: About the size of a pigeon — 25–30 cm — but surprisingly compact and round
hints.behavior: Dives underwater and can carry a dozen small fish crosswise in its bill at once, flying back to its burrow to feed chicks

funFact: Puffins are excellent swimmers, using their wings to 'fly' underwater at depths of up to 60 metres.

facts.size: About 25–30 cm — slightly larger than an American Robin
facts.habitat: Mixed and deciduous forests, parks, and suburban areas
facts.diet: Acorns, seeds, nuts, insects, and occasionally eggs of other birds
facts.migration: Partially migratory; some populations move south in autumn

hints.region: Common across eastern and central North America, from southern Canada to Florida
hints.size: A bit larger than a robin — around 25–30 cm, bold and upright in posture
hints.behavior: Known for its loud, raucous calls and its ability to mimic the calls of Red-tailed Hawks to scare off competitors

funFact: Blue Jays play a key ecological role by caching thousands of acorns each autumn — many of which sprout into oak trees.

facts.size: 120–145 cm tall — one of the tallest wading birds
facts.habitat: Shallow saltwater lagoons, estuaries, and mudflats
facts.diet: Algae, brine shrimp, and small invertebrates filtered from the water
facts.migration: Partially migratory; populations move in response to water levels and food availability

hints.region: Found in large flocks around shallow salty lakes and lagoons across southern Europe, Africa, and Asia
hints.size: One of the tallest wading birds — standing up to 145 cm on long, spindly legs
hints.behavior: Feeds by holding its distinctive bent bill upside-down in the water, filtering tiny organisms through a specialised comb-like structure

funFact: Flamingos get their pink colour from the carotenoid pigments in the algae and crustaceans they eat — without this diet, they would be white.

facts.size: Body 100–120 cm; male train up to 160 cm long
facts.habitat: Forests, grasslands, and farmland; also common around villages
facts.diet: Seeds, insects, lizards, small snakes, and fruit

hints.region: Native to the Indian subcontinent; introduced widely across the world in parks and estates
hints.size: A large, ground-dwelling bird — the body alone is over a metre long, with the male's trailing display plumage adding another 160 cm
hints.behavior: Males spread and shake their brilliant iridescent tail fans in elaborate courtship displays, vibrating feathers to produce an infrasonic rumble

funFact: The 'tail' feathers of a peacock are actually elongated upper tail coverts — the real tail beneath acts as a support strut.

facts.size: About 42–55 cm, including the large bill
facts.diet: Fruit (especially figs and berries), insects, small lizards, and eggs
facts.migration: Non-migratory; may make short altitudinal movements

hints.region: Found in the humid lowland forests of southern Mexico through Central America to Colombia and Venezuela
hints.size: About 50 cm long — roughly the size of a crow — but the enormous bill accounts for a third of that length
hints.behavior: Uses its long, lightweight bill to pluck fruit and toss it into the air, catching it at the back of the throat to swallow

funFact: Despite its enormous appearance, the toucan's bill is mostly hollow keratin — extremely lightweight and also helps regulate body temperature.

facts.habitat: Open Arctic tundra; moves to open farmland, airports, and coasts in winter
facts.diet: Mainly lemmings; also voles, rabbits, and birds
facts.migration: Irruptive migrant — numbers moving south vary dramatically year to year with lemming population cycles

hints.region: Breeds on the Arctic tundra; in winter, individuals drift south to open country across Canada, northern USA, and northern Europe
hints.size: One of the largest owls by weight — 53–65 cm tall with a wingspan exceeding 1.5 metres
hints.behavior: Unlike most owls, hunts by day as well as night on the treeless tundra; a single owl can eat over 1,600 lemmings per year

funFact: Female Snowy Owls are larger than males and more heavily barred with brown — a reversal of the typical size difference in most bird of prey species.

facts.habitat: Deciduous woodlands, hedgerows, parks, and gardens
facts.migration: Mostly resident in western Europe; eastern populations migrate to the Mediterranean in winter

hints.region: Found across Europe and western Asia, from Scandinavia to the Azores — one of the most familiar garden birds in Britain
hints.size: Palm-sized at about 14 cm — much smaller than most people imagine when they first see one up close
hints.behavior: Aggressively territorial year-round; famously follows gardeners, dashing forward to grab worms disturbed by digging

funFact: The European Robin's melodious song can be heard on Christmas Eve and into the small hours of the morning — they are one of the few European birds to sing at night.

facts.size: About 16–17 cm — roughly the size of a sparrow
facts.habitat: Clear, slow-moving rivers and streams; also ponds and coasts in winter
facts.diet: Small fish, aquatic insects, and occasionally tadpoles
facts.migration: Mostly resident; some northern birds move south or to sheltered coasts in hard winters

hints.region: Found along clear waterways across Europe, Asia, and North Africa — look for a fast-moving electric-blue flash low over the water
hints.size: Surprisingly small — roughly sparrow-sized at 16–17 cm, though its vivid colour makes it appear larger
hints.behavior: Perches motionless on a branch above the water, then dives beak-first to catch fish, always returning to the same perch to stun and swallow its prey

funFact: The Common Kingfisher's iridescent blue back feathers contain no blue pigment at all — the colour is produced entirely by the microscopic structure of the feather barbs scattering light.

facts.size: About 21–23 cm — similar to an American Robin but slimmer
facts.habitat: Woodland edges, shrubby gardens, thickets, and hedgerows

hints.region: Common across eastern and central North America, from southern Canada to northern Mexico; expanding its range northward
hints.size: About 22 cm long — comparable to a robin in length, with a prominent pointed crest
hints.behavior: Unlike most birds, female Northern Cardinals also sing — pairs often counter-sing to each other to communicate food needs or predator alerts

funFact: The Northern Cardinal is the official state bird of seven US states — more than any other species.

facts.size: 100–130 cm tall — the tallest and heaviest penguin species
facts.habitat: Antarctic sea ice and surrounding Southern Ocean
facts.diet: Fish, squid, and krill caught by diving
facts.migration: Non-migratory; travels on foot or by swimming between feeding grounds and breeding colonies

hints.region: Lives on and around Antarctica — the only penguin species that breeds during the Antarctic winter
hints.size: The largest penguin — up to 130 cm tall and weighing up to 45 kg
hints.behavior: Males incubate a single egg on their feet through the brutal Antarctic winter, huddling in large groups, fasting for up to four months until the chick hatches

funFact: Emperor Penguins can dive to depths of over 550 metres and hold their breath for more than 20 minutes — deeper and longer than any other bird.

facts.size: About 8–9 cm — roughly the length of your thumb
facts.habitat: Open woodlands, forest edges, meadows, and gardens with flowering plants
facts.diet: Nectar from flowers and small insects for protein
facts.migration: Long-distance migrant — crosses the Gulf of Mexico non-stop to winter in Central America

hints.region: The only hummingbird that breeds in eastern North America; summers from Canada to the Gulf Coast, winters in Central America
hints.size: Tiny — about 8–9 cm, no longer than your thumb, and weighing less than a 5-cent coin
hints.behavior: Hovers in place by beating its wings up to 53 times per second; the only bird that can fly backwards and upside down

funFact: Despite weighing just 3 grams, Ruby-throated Hummingbirds fly non-stop across the Gulf of Mexico — a 900 km journey — during migration.

facts.size: 44–55 cm — about the size of a large crow
facts.habitat: Forests, woodlands, and increasingly urban parks and gardens
facts.diet: Seeds, berries, nuts, roots, and leaf buds

hints.region: Native to Australia and New Guinea; noisy flocks are a familiar sight across Australian cities and bushland
hints.size: About 50 cm — crow-sized — with a dramatic, fan-shaped yellow crest it raises when alarmed or excited
hints.behavior: Highly intelligent and social; known for making significant noise in large flocks at dawn and dusk, and for its ability to mimic speech in captivity

funFact: Sulphur-crested Cockatoos can live for 70 years or more in captivity, making them a true lifetime commitment as a pet.

facts.habitat: Open farmland, grassland, and woodland edges; often nests in old barns and church towers
facts.diet: Small mammals — especially voles, mice, and shrews
facts.migration: Mostly resident; a sedentary species rarely moving far from its territory

hints.region: One of the most widespread land birds on Earth — found on every continent except Antarctica and most of the Arctic
hints.size: Medium-sized owl — about 35 cm tall with a wingspan approaching a metre, but very lightweight at only 250–350 g
hints.behavior: Hunts entirely in darkness by sound — its asymmetric ear placement gives it the ability to locate prey with millimetre accuracy without any light at all

funFact: The Barn Owl's heart-shaped facial disc acts like a satellite dish, channelling sound waves to its ears — its hearing is the most sensitive of any animal ever tested.
